paragraphs.text: Where policy meets pressure. A worldwide map of marine protection, the 30×30 target, and four decades of ocean governance — read as a single response system.
NOAA MPA INVENTORY · IUCN WDPA 2024 · UN BBNJ AGREEMENT · MARINE CONSERVATION INST. ATLAS

Each hexagon represents one of the world's largest marine protected areas, sized equally for comparison and colored by enforcement status. The teal hexes — those with active monitoring, no-take zones, and adequate funding — total fewer than a third of the count.
The shortfall between today's fully-protected coverage and the 2030 target. Closing it would require designating an area equivalent to all of Africa's landmass — annually — for six years.
Defined EEZs, continental shelves, and the "common heritage" principle for the deep seabed. 168 parties.
First legal mechanism to create MPAs in the 64% of ocean beyond national jurisdiction. Needs 60 ratifications to enter force.
Slow steaming, scrubber bans, low-sulphur fuel mandates across 14 major routes.
Mangrove and seagrass replanting across 31 coastal nations, sequestering CO₂ at 4× forest rates.
Argo float network expanded to 3,950 autonomous profilers across all major basins.
Quota cuts, gear bans, observer coverage. Bottom-trawl moratoria expanded in 9 EEZs.
EPR plastic mandates ratified in 47 countries. Industrial discharge permits tightened.
Locally-Managed Marine Areas in 14 Pacific & Indian Ocean nations. 1.4M co-stewards.
Quarterly briefings on MPA designations, treaty ratifications, and the data behind ocean response zones. No spam, no algorithms.
We publish open methodology, share raw datasets via DOI, and co-produce infographic stories with reef monitoring programs, fisheries observers, and Indigenous marine stewards worldwide.
A non-profit infographic journal translating NOAA and peer-reviewed ocean science into visual data stories. Published quarterly. Open data, open methodology.

items.text:
Current Coverage vs. 2030 Target
            8.2% PROTECTED · 21.8% REMAINING
If the current annual designation rate of ~0.4 percentage points per year continues, the 30% target will not be reached until 2078 — 48 years behind schedule.

Subscribe to the Current
          Quarterly briefings on MPA designations, treaty ratifications, and the data behind ocean response zones. No spam, no algorithms.

Partner With Deep Current
          We publish open methodology, share raw datasets via DOI, and co-produce infographic stories with reef monitoring programs, fisheries observers, and Indigenous marin

Milestone Treaties & Declarations · 1982 → 2023
            8 EVENTS · 41 YEAR SPAN
UNCLOS (1982) established the territorial framework. BBNJ (2023) closed the high-seas governance gap, allowing protected designations beyond national jurisdiction for the first time.

visibleText: Deep Current Ocean Data Journal Ocean Health Coral Systems Plastic Tides Temperature Rise Action Atlas Vol.05 / Conservation Response Action Atlas . Where policy meets pressure. A worldwide map of marine protection, the 30×30 target, and four decades of ocean governance — read as a single response system. ● 48 MPAs Mapped ● UN 30×30 Goal ● Treaty Timeline Total MPA Area 28.1M km² Fully Protected 8.2 % 2030 Target 30 % Signatory Nations 87 On This Page MPA Hex Grid UN 30×30 Target Policy Timeline Six Actions Partners & Stats Take Action Protection Key Fully protected Partially protected Paper park Unprotected EEZ Ocean Depth 0 M −1K −2K −4K −6K −11K Source NOAA MPA INVENTORY · IUCN WDPA 2024 · UN BBNJ AGREEMENT · MARINE CONSERVATION INST. ATLAS § 01 / Global Inventory The 48 MPA Hex Grid FIG 1.1 Each hexagon represents one of the world's largest marine protected areas, sized equally for comparison and colored by enforcement status. The teal hexes — those with active monitoring, no-take zones, and adequate funding — total fewer than a third of the count. Marine Protected Areas Worldwide, 2024 N = 48 · GROUPED BY OCEAN BASIN PROJECTION · EQUAL-AREA HEX PACIFIC ATLANTIC INDIAN SOUTHERN OCEAN PAPA PIPA PRI SAR AZR ASC MAL SOC NWH REV COC BLZ BER CAB CHA CCH GAL RAP FER STH TRI MAY SEY PAL SOL FIJ NCL VAN CHG MAU KER MCQ TAS FAL SGE PEI KRG HRD ROS AMU WED EAA DOL BCA PEN TOTAL HEXES 48 · BASINS 4 Fully protected · 22 Partial · 18 Paper park · 8 Paper parks are zones designated on map but lacking enforcement, funding, or no-take regulation; together they cover ~3.4M km² of nominal protection. SOURCE · MARINE CONSERVATION INSTITUTE MPATLAS, 2024-Q2 No-Take Zones 2.9% OF GLOBAL OCEAN Largest MPA 1.51M km² ROSS SEA · ANTARCTICA High Seas Treaty 87/193 NATIONS SIGNED · 22 RATIFIED § 02 / UN Biodiversity Framework The 30×30 Gap FIG 2.1 Current Coverage vs. 2030 Target 8.2% PROTECTED · 21.8% REMAINING CURRENT 8.2% TARGET 30% BY 2030 0% 25% 50% 75% Nation km² % Global MPA ● United States 3,200,000 11.4 ● France (Overseas) 2,400,000 8.5 ● United Kingdom 1,800,000 6.4 ● Australia 1,650,000 5.9 ●  Chile 1,440,000 5.1 ●  Other 82 Nations 17,610,000 62.7 If the current annual designation rate of ~0.4 percentage points per year continues, the 30% target will not be reached until 2078 — 48 years behind schedule. Headline Gap 21.8 pts The shortfall between today's fully-protected coverage and the 2030 target. Closing it would require designating an area equivalent to all of Africa's landmass — annually — for six years. § 03 / Policy Timeline Forty Years of Ocean Law FIG 3.1 Milestone Treaties & Declarations · 1982 → 2023 8 EVENTS · 41 YEAR SPAN 1982 1992 2002 2012 2022 2030 1982 UNCLOS 1992 CBD RIO 1995 FISH STOCKS 2002 WSSD MPA NET 2010 AICHI TARGETS 2015 SDG 14 2022 GBF · 30×30 2023 HIGH SEAS TREATY 2030 DEADLINE UNCLOS (1982) established the territorial framework. BBNJ (2023) closed the high-seas governance gap, allowing protected designations beyond national jurisdiction for the first time. UNCLOS · 1982 EIF 1994 Defined EEZs, continental shelves, and the "common heritage" principle for the deep seabed. 168 parties. BBNJ High Seas Treaty · 2023 RATIFICATION ONGOING First legal mechanism to create MPAs in the 64% of ocean beyond national jurisdiction. Needs 60 ratifications to enter force. § 04 / Response Categories Six Levers of Action FIG 4.1–4.6 Action 01 Sustainable Shipping Slow steaming, scrubber bans, low-sulphur fuel mandates across 14 major routes. −24% SOx since 2020 Action 02 Blue Carbon Restoration Mangrove and seagrass replanting across 31 coastal nations, sequestering CO₂ at 4× forest rates. 182k ha restored Action 03 Research & Monitoring Argo float network expanded to 3,950 autonomous profilers across all major basins. 3,950 active floats Action 04 Fisheries Reform Quota cuts, gear bans, observer coverage. Bottom-trawl moratoria expanded in 9 EEZs. −18% bycatch '21–'24 Action 05 Land-Source Pollution EPR plastic mandates ratified in 47 countries. Industrial discharge permits tightened. 47 EPR laws active Action 06 Community Stewardship Locally-Managed Marine Areas in 14 Pacific & Indian Ocean nations. 1.4M co-stewards. 1.4M stewards engaged § 05 / Partnership Network Who Moves the Needle FIG 5.1 Funding Flow by Sector · 2024 USD 14.6B GLOBAL OCEAN CONSERVATION SPEND $1B $2B $3B $4B $5B MULTILATERAL $3.89B PHILANTHROPY $3.18B NATIONAL GOVT $2.71B CORPORATE $1.86B ACADEMIC $1.36B NGO / COMMUNITY $0.96B INDIGENOUS-LED $0.66B Indigenous-led conservation receives the smallest sector allocation despite stewarding ~30% of ocean biodiversity hotspots. Multilateral funds (GEF, World Bank, GCF) drive the largest share. SOURCE · OUR SHARED SEAS, OCEAN FUNDING TRACKER 2024 Partner Orgs 312 ACROSS 94 NATIONS Volunteer Hours 4.1M LOGGED 2023 Cleanup Tonnage 88k METRIC TONS · COASTAL Citizen Reports 612k VIA IFISH & SEAGRASSWATCH "The ocean has no constituency, no vote, no border patrol — only treaties, only us. The next six years decide whether 30×30 becomes a milestone or a missed exit." — Dr. Lena Okonkwo · UN Ocean Envoy § 06 / Take Action Join the Atlas CTA Subscribe to the Current Quarterly briefings on MPA designations, treaty ratifications, and the data behind ocean response zones. No spam, no algorithms.
